name: Lisbon Valley
short_description: BLM rockhounding area in southeast Utah known for uranium mineral specimens and colorful agate and jasper finds. Open for free public collecting with standard BLM limits.
description:
Lisbon Valley is a productive BLM rockhounding destination in San Juan County, Utah, famous among mineral collectors for its uranium-bearing minerals and secondary uranium compounds. Located in the heart of the Colorado Plateau, this area has a rich mining history tied to Cold War-era uranium extraction, and today visitors can still find specimens in weathered outcrops and wash gravels.

The valley sits within a Jurassic and Cretaceous sedimentary formation where uranium was concentrated through natural geochemical processes over millions of years. Collectors typically find bright yellow, orange, and green uranium minerals including autunite, torbernite, and carnotite specimens, often in association with silica-rich agate and jasper. The mineralization is accessible without special equipment and rewards both experienced collectors and curious beginners.

The terrain is semi-arid high desert with scattered pinyon and juniper vegetation. Collecting is relatively straightforward - many specimens weather out of hillside outcrops and accumulate in nearby washes after seasonal runoff. The area's remoteness means fewer crowds than many Utah rockhounding sites, though this also means limited services nearby.

Expect to spend 2-4 hours searching productive areas. Bring plenty of water, sunscreen, and a hand lens to identify mineral specimens. The best collecting is done in the early morning or late afternoon to avoid intense midday heat. While uranium minerals are present, they occur in specimen quantities suitable for collection and display, not in hazardous concentrations for casual rockhounders.
history: Lisbon Valley lies within the uranium mineralization belt of the Colorado Plateau, where Late Jurassic Morrison Formation sandstones and associated mudstones concentrated uranium through groundwater circulation during the Tertiary period. The area saw intense prospecting and mining activity during the 1950s-1970s uranium boom, when government incentives drove hundreds of small claims and larger mining operations across the region.
tips: Search along the sides of hillside outcrops where weathering exposes bright yellow and green uranium minerals; these often occur as coatings or small crystal aggregates on rock surfaces. Use a UV lamp at night to make autunite and other fluorescent uranium minerals glow, which greatly speeds identification and collection. Focus your search in washes and drainages where gravity has concentrated loose specimens over decades. Bring a hand lens and field guide specific to uranium minerals - positive identification will improve your success rate significantly.
terrain_notes: Lisbon Valley sits at approximately 6,000 feet elevation in high desert terrain with scattered pinyon-juniper woodland and exposed Jurassic-age sandstone and mudstone outcrops. The landscape is gently rolling to hilly with moderate slopes. Washes and small drainages are common features where weathered material collects. Most collecting areas are within 1-3 miles of vehicle access, requiring 20-45 minutes of walking over uneven rocky ground. Footing is moderately rough with loose rocks and unstable slopes common in some areas.
written_directions: From Monticello, take US 191 south for approximately 10 miles. Turn east onto CR 115 (also known as Lisbon Valley Road) and follow it for about 8-12 miles into the valley. The road is unpaved but generally passable for high-clearance passenger vehicles in good weather. Look for exposed sandstone hillsides and washes on both sides of the road. Park in pullouts near promising outcrops. A detailed Utah BLM map or GPS coordinates are highly recommended as road intersections are not always clearly marked.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt road, may be washboard and rough in sections. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Passable for standard vehicles in dry conditions but can be muddy or impassable after heavy rain or snowmelt.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along Lisbon Valley Road near exposed hillsides and washes. No developed parking facilities. Space available for 3-6 vehicles at most locations.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or removal of material from cliff faces. Surface collection from washes and loose material only. Respect all claim markers and private property boundaries.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sale
No motorized excavation
Surface collection only - no digging or prying from rock faces
Respect mining claim markers
No off-road vehicle use outside designated roads
nearest_services: Monticello, Utah - approximately 15-20 miles west. Gas, groceries, restaurants, and lodging available. Last reliable water and supplies before heading into the valley.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose rocks common. No wheelchair access. Hiking required to reach most productive areas. Moderate elevation (6,000 feet) should be manageable for most hikers, but the desert sun and limited shade can be challenging. Sturdy boots with good ankle support recommended. Most areas suitable for families with school-age children comfortable with 1-3 mile hikes on uneven ground.

faq.question: What uranium minerals can I find rockhounding at Lisbon Valley, Utah?
faq.answer: Lisbon Valley is known for bright yellow and green uranium minerals including autunite, torbernite, and carnotite. These typically appear as crystal coatings or small aggregates on weathered sandstone outcrops. A UV lamp makes autunite glow under ultraviolet light, making identification much easier. You may also find associated agate and jasper specimens in the same areas.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at Lisbon Valley BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Lisbon Valley. As BLM public land, it allows free personal use collection under standard FLPMA regulations - 25 lbs per day maximum, no commercial collection, surface collecting only. Always check current BLM website for any temporary closures or restrictions before your trip.

faq.question: What is the best season to go rockhounding near Monticello at Lisbon Valley?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Utah's high desert location means summer heat can be extreme (90-100+ degrees F), while winter may bring snow to 6,000-foot elevations. Fall and spring offer mild temperatures and lower water needs. Morning and late afternoon are best to avoid intense midday sun regardless of season.

faq.question: Is Lisbon Valley rockhounding suitable for families with kids and dogs?
faq.answer: Yes, Lisbon Valley is beginner and family-friendly. The terrain is moderately difficult with uneven ground, making it suitable for kids age 8 and up comfortable with hiking. Dogs are allowed on BLM land. Most productive areas are within 1-3 miles of parking. Bring plenty of water for everyone - shade is limited in the high desert.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding uranium minerals at Lisbon Valley?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens or 10x magnifier for identifying small specimens, a UV lamp for nighttime fluorescence testing, and a field guide to uranium minerals. A sturdy backpack, water (at least 3 liters per person), sunscreen, hat, and comfortable hiking boots are essential. A small pick or pry bar helps extract specimens from soft sandstone, but motorized tools are not allowed.

faq.question: How far is Lisbon Valley from Monticello and what is the road access like?
faq.answer: Lisbon Valley is 15-20 miles southeast of Monticello. From US 191, take unpaved Lisbon Valley Road (CR 115) east for 8-12 miles into the valley. The road is rough and unpaved but passable for high-clearance vehicles in dry conditions. After rain or snowmelt it can become impassable. Check road conditions before driving and allow 45-60 minutes from town to reach productive areas.
